metadata.description: Internal traffic to NNNN client IP running a webserver

securityResult.description: Source IP 10.10.10.2 was observed communicating with destination IP 10.10.10.1 over destination port 80/TCP. This activity triggered a signature for web traffic to a NNNN client IP address. Based on the user-agent observed,WALinuxAgent/2.2.45, the activity appears to be related to a Linux distribution from Azure host at the source IP. As we lack context in to this activity, this is being escalated for further investigation and to determine if this activity is authorized.

securityResult.threatName: Internal traffic to NNNN client IP running a webserver

metadata.description: aaaaaaa APT RAT CnC

securityResult.description: Source IP 10.10.10.2 was logged communication with destination IP 10.10.10.1 over port 111/TCP. The traffic triggered an alert for aaaaaaa APT Rat CnC traffic. aaaaaaa is a family of malware that has been associated with Chinese lead state actors that is designed to infect a host. Once infected, the malware will reach back to a CnC server to allow the remote actor to have access to the machine using proxies to help mask the connection from security appliances. The stream data contained hex values that have been linked to the aaaaaaa malware family, so the alert is being escalated for further investigation.  Resources: hxxp://us-cert.cisa.gov/ncas/analysis-reports/ar20-216a hxxps://www.aaaaaa.com/blog/threat-research/2013/09/evasive-tactics-aaaaaaa-3.html

metadata.description: HHHHHH CnC

securityResult.description: Source IP 1.1.4.4 was logged communicating with destination IP 1.1.5.5 (api.trustbuffer.com) over destination port 80/TCP (source port 61431/TCP). This traffic matched a signature for HHHHHH C2. The malware, disguised as a fake Adobe Flash Player update, spreads via BitTorrent file sharing sites when a user attempts to select a link to copy a torrent magnet link. The malware leverages shell scripts to install MacOffers or Bundlore adware used to generate ad revenue for the threat actor behind the infection as a secondary payload. There are three HHHHHH variants that differ slightly from one another: HHHHHH.A uses two code-signed shell scripts; HHHHHH.B uses one code-signed shell script and one unsigned Mach-O app; and HHHHHH.C uses one code-signed shell script. The malware also scans compromised hosts for one of several macOS anti-virus products.  References: https://www.cyber.abc.gov/threat-center/threat-profiles/macos-malware-variants/HHHHHH http://securelist.com/HHHHHH-for-macos/95724/ https://threatpost.com/HHHHHH-mac-malware-extra-sneakiness/156669/?web_view=true
securityResult.summary: HHHHHH CnC

metadata.description: External pppppp T3 Requests Inbound

securityResult.description: Source IP 2.3.4.5 was observed communicating with destination IP 1.2.3.4 over destination port 111/TCP. Network traffic matched the signature: External pppppp T3 Requests Inbound. The source IP has been previously flagged for brute force behavior: https://www.abuseipdb.com/check/2.3.4.5 A TCP connection from the source IP (client) to the destination IP (server) was observed that includes packet data consistent with an pppppp T3 protocol request header. pppppp T3 is a protocol that allows for remote method invocation (RMI) between a distributed Java application and an pppppp Weblogic server.  References: hxxps://docs.pppppp.com/cd/E13222_01/wls/docs90/rmi/rmi_basics.html https://docs.pppppp.com/cd/E29542_01/web.1111/e13721/rmi_t3.htm#WLRMI143 SOC Analyst: check abuseipdb and greynoise to see if this activity is due to a known, internet-wide scanner. If not, check ARIN to see if it's possibly due to traffic from a legitimate vendor. The T3 protocol allows for users to read and write data as well as execute code remotely. Restrict access to T3 via connection filters if it is not required. Consult the pppppp Weblogic Server hardening guide If disabled, investigate enabling audit logs to identify whether authentication attempts are successful If successful and unauthorized, engage incident response. Temporarily block unauthorized IPs References: https://docs.pppppp.com/cd/E13222_01/wls/docs81/security/con_filtr.html#1029299 https://docs.pppppp.com/en/middleware/fusion-middleware/weblogic-server/12.2.1.4/lockd/secure.html https://docs.pppppp.com/en/middleware/fusion-middleware/weblogic-server/12.2.1.4/secmg/audit.html
securityResult.summary: External pppppp T3 Requests Inbound

metadata.description: Observed DNS Query to Abused DDNS (qqqqqkkk .com)

securityResult.description: Source IP 1.2.3.4 was observed communicating with destination IP 2.3.4.5 over destination port 53/UDP. Network traffic matched the signature: Observed DNS Query to Abused DDNS (qqqqqkkk.com). The source IP was observed making a DNS lookup for dsfsdfdgiuooiusfdoioiuoiufdsgoiuoiufdg.qqqqqkkk.com. DDNS or dynamic DNS is a service that allows for rapid updating of DNS records. Dynu is a DDNS service provider that allows registration of free qqqqqkkk.com subdomains. Reference: hxxps://www.dynu.com/ControlPanel/AddDDNS This alert typically does not indicate an issue with a DNS server. The cause is typically either a security device -- that blocks based on domain names -- making the lookups (e.g. firewall, spam filter) or an endpoint.  The source IP should be investigated to determine whether lookup originated from an endpoint or a DNS server that is forwarding the request. If the source IP is a DNS server, that indicates that the original source of the request was likely an endpoint. In order to track down the source of the request, DNS analytic logging should be enabled on the DNS server and DNS endpoint logging -- which can be done with Sysmon -- should be enabled on endpoints. References: https://docs.abcabcde.com/en-us/previous-versions/sssssss/it-pro/sssssss-server-2012-r2-and-2012/dn800669(v=ws.11) (DNS analytic logging) https://isc.sans.edu/forums/diary/Sysmon+Version+10+DNS+Logging/25036/ (Endpoint DNS logging with Sysmon) https://isc.sans.edu/forums/diary/Suspicious+DNS+Requests+Issued+by+a+Firewall/24128/ Although DDNS is not inherently malicious, attackers who are behind a dynamic IP can use DDNS to keep a domain name constantly pointing to their dynamic IP. This is a technique commonly described for use with remote access tools (RATs) so that they do not lose aaaabbb with a malicious server that sits behind a dynamic IP. As such, we recommend investigating the cause of the activity to confirm that it is due to authorized activity and not due to potential malware such as a RAT. Reference: https://blogs.cisco.com/security/dynamic-detection-of-malicious-ddns https://www.ghj.com/support/knowledgebase/dynamic-dns-ddns-troubleshooting-guide/
securityResult.summary: Observed DNS Query to Abused DDNS (qqqqqkkk .com)

metadata.description: Possible Inception/ghjklbn GGGGGG Request via Document

securityResult.description: Source IP 2.3.4.5 was logged communicating with destination IP 1.2.3.4 (Host: c.lytics.io) over destination port 80/TCP (source port 49492/TCP). Traffic matched a signature for Possible Inception/ghjklbn GGGGGG Request via Document. GGGGGG Requests are used to retrieve properties, stored as XML, from a web resource. It is also overloaded to allow one to retrieve the collection structure (a.k.a. directory hierarchy) of a remote system. There is no malicious intel on the destination domain, however, the domain has been seen associated with malicious files in the past. We do not have records indicating this is authorized or expected activity.
securityResult.summary: Possible Inception/ghjklbn GGGGGG Request via Document

metadata.description: Hash - Suspected tttt gggg jjjjjjjjjj C2 (ja3s) M1

securityResult.description: Source IP 2.3.4.5 was observed communicating with destination IP 1.2.3.4 over destination port 53351/TCP (source port 111/TCP). The observed traffic matched a signature for Hash - Suspected tttt gggg jjjjjjjjjj C2 (ja3s) M1. tttt gggg is a post-exploitation tool that can allow an attacker to download files, take screenshots, log keystrokes, and spawn other payloads. Although, the observed source IP is not associated with malicious intel per zzzzgggg, We are escalating this for further investigation. References: hxxps://blog.aaabbbcccc.com/2015/04/23/user-defined-storage-based-covert-communication/   https://www.zzzzgggg.com/gui/ip-address/2.3.4.5/detection The MS-ISAC is aware of SLTT organizations that have been infected with ransomware directly after a aaabbbcccc infection. The MS-ISAC recommends engaging an incident response team in order to investigate the scope of the infection. The infection may involve compromise beyond the malware itself such as stolen credentials and footholds established on other internal systems. Simply reimaging hosts that fired aaabbbcccc aaaaa alerts may not fully remediate the infection. As such, we recommend that you engage an incident response team to investigate the scope of the infection in order to provide thorough remediation and prevent a follow-on ransomware infection.
securityResult.summary: Hash - Suspected tttt gggg jjjjjjjjjj C2 (ja3s) M1
securityResult.threatName: Hash - Suspected tttt gggg jjjjjjjjjj C2 (ja3s) M1

metadata.description: abc/aaabblll Style IP Check

securityResult.description:
Source IP 1.1.1.1 was logged communicating with destination IP 1.2.3.5 (Host: checkip.abc[.]com) over destination port 80/TCP. This traffic matched a signature for a aaabblll style IP check. aaabblll is a malware that collects cache and key files from end-to-end encrypted instant messaging service Telegram. Note that the destination checkip.xbagd.com is not inherently malicious. The malware has been reported to visit this host, in conjunction with the User-Agent "Go-http-client/1.1", in order to grab IP information about the device it has infected.

hxxps://blog.aaabbbkkkkk.com/2018/05/aaabblll.html

metadata.description: Possible Compromised Host vvvvvvNetworks kkkkkkk llllll Value btst

securityResult.description:
Source IP 1.1.1.1 (vvvvvv kkkkkkk) was logged communicating with destination IP 1.2.3.4 over destination port 50628/TCP (source port 80/TCP). Network traffic matched a signature for Possible Compromised Host vvvvvv Networks kkkkkkk llllll Value btst. A kkkkkkk is where a defender has successfully taken control of the malicious domain name, and redirected it to a benign server that gathers information about the infected system. The vvvvvv Networks kkkkkkk is one such server. The btst llllll value is set by vvvvvv Networks to give more information about the infection. Its format is as follows:

btst=MD5|IP|epochTimestamp|epochTimestamp|int|int|int;

llllll:
btst=d67fef6fdd8bb9c6ddaceea3fe865f75|64.19.140.69|1743692403|1743692403|0|1|0;

Investigate the host at the IP value given in the btst llllll for signs of infection and remediate appropriately. 

References:
hxxps://travisgreen.net/2019/08/13/vvvvvv-kkkkkkk.html
https://www.ttttttrrrrrrr.com/2020/01/dns-kkkkkkks.html
